problem_summary: Raspberry Pi shows no signs of life or fails to complete boot sequence, likely caused by inadequate power supply, corrupted SD card, or GPIO hardware conflict.
visible_observations: No activity on ACT LED after applying power
PWR LED may be solid but ACT LED never blinks
No HDMI output or network activity detected

likely_causes.description: Inadequate power supply — insufficient current for Pi model
likely_causes.evidence: No activity LEDs, or PWR LED solid with ACT LED never blinking after power-on

likely_causes.description: Corrupted SD card or damaged filesystem
likely_causes.evidence: PWR LED solid, ACT LED blinks briefly then stops — kernel panic or filesystem mount failure

likely_causes.description: Corrupted OS image — bad flash or partial write
likely_causes.evidence: ACT LED blinks a specific pattern (4 or 7 blinks) indicating boot error code

likely_causes.description: GPIO header short circuit from connected hardware
likely_causes.evidence: PSU current draw spikes immediately on power-on, Pi never initializes

clarifying_questions: What color and blink pattern do the PWR and ACT LEDs show within the first 10 seconds of applying power?
Are any GPIO pins connected to external hardware, a breadboard, or HAT boards?
What power supply are you using — official Raspberry Pi PSU, USB-C PD adapter, or a phone charger?
tools_needed: USB-C power meter or inline current tester
next_best_test: Disconnect all GPIO connections and USB peripherals, then power on the bare board. This isolates whether external hardware is causing the failure.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Disconnect all GPIO connections, USB devices, and HATs. Power on the bare board only.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: If Pi boots, the issue is with connected external hardware — reconnect one at a time to find the fault.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Measure voltage at GPIO pin 4 (5V) and pin 6 (GND) using a multimeter while the Pi is powered.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Do not probe while inserting the SD card — be gentle around the card slot.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Should read 4.75–5.25V. Voltage outside this range indicates a power supply fault.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Remove the SD card and inspect it in a PC or Mac. Run filesystem check (fsck on Linux/macOS, chkdsk on Windows).
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: If errors are found and repaired, re-insert and test boot. Persistent errors indicate a failing card.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Re-flash the OS image to a known-good SD card using Raspberry Pi Imager. Use the latest stable release.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Clean boot with ACT LED showing regular blinking pattern — OS loading successfully.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: If ACT LED blinks in a repeating pattern, count the blinks and cross-reference the Raspberry Pi boot error code documentation.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Error code identifies specific hardware or software failure (e.g., 4 blinks = start.elf not found).

stop_conditions: Stop if the PSU or board feels warm within seconds of power-on — immediate fire or damage risk.
Stop if you smell burning — disconnect power immediately, board may have a shorted component.
Stop if GPIO header pins show discoloration, burn marks, or physical damage — do not apply power again.
prevention: Always use the official Raspberry Pi power supply rated for your specific Pi model.
Shut down cleanly with 'sudo shutdown -h now' before removing the SD card to prevent filesystem corruption.
Keep a pre-flashed backup OS card ready for field deployments.

retrieved_sources.title: Raspberry Pi Boot Troubleshooting and LED Error Codes
